danger_signals.visual_form: Animated enemy sprites with distinct visual designs distinct from Yumetaro
danger_signals.meaning: Threat to player; contact causes damage

danger_signals.visual_form: Large, detailed sprite occupying significant screen real estate; typically positioned at level end
danger_signals.meaning: Major threat requiring defeat to progress; heightened difficulty

danger_signals.visual_form: Background and foreground obstacles (spikes, gaps, moving platforms) visually distinct from safe passage
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental damage or fall death; requires platforming precision

danger_signals.visual_form: HUD display of remaining lives decrements; visual feedback on character damage/knockback
danger_signals.meaning: Player resource depletion; game over condition approaching

safety_signals.visual_form: Spawning star projectile above Yumetaro's horn solidifies into platform; bright, glowing appearance
safety_signals.meaning: Player-controlled safe passage; accessible area created on demand

safety_signals.visual_form: Magic items glow/shimmer in dedicated stage locations; distinct visual presentation from enemies
safety_signals.meaning: Progress toward good ending; hidden collectible secured

safety_signals.visual_form: Stage advancement markers; transition between level sections
safety_signals.meaning: Safe progression; player advancement through world

safety_signals.visual_form: Character sprite animated and responsive to input; horn glowing when star-attack ready
safety_signals.meaning: Player agency intact; combat/mobility capability available

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Yumetaro's presence marks player-controlled safe zone; stars mark his attack area

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Present/toy form in opening; human form in ending cutscene
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Depicted in intro/outro cinematics; presence motivates all player action

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Variety of distinct enemy designs (Ohagi, etc.); non-Yumetaro sprite forms
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Different enemy types per stage; behavior varies but all oppose player
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Scattered throughout levels; concentrated in final castle area
Progressive architectural decay; final stage shows castle collapse sequence

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Colorful, diverse environments; toy-themed aesthetics; varied background art per stage
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies have moderate patrol patterns; star attack sufficient for basic progression

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Environments become darker, more castle-like; architectural elements dominate; visual complexity increases
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy density increases; boss patterns require learned attack timings; star platforming mechanics become mandatory

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Bad ending state (defeat without magic items)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Loop restart; same stages replay from beginning
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: No access to secret Stage 7; final boss remains inaccessible; cycle repeats

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Good ending state (all magic items collected, no continues)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Secret Stage 7 revealed; final boss arena accessible; castle destruction cutscene triggers upon victory
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player gains access to true final boss; rescue cutscene plays; world narrative completes

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Anti-tampering message displayed referencing Atlantis no Nazo; progression halt at Stage 7
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game prevents access to final boss; blocks good ending achievement

environmental_storytelling.example: Stages 1-3 show toy shop and natural environments; Stages 4-6 transition into crumbling castle with visibly damaged walls, debris, and ominous atmosphere
Stage-specific enemy designs (Ohagi in ship interior Stage 2) suggest location-appropriate hostile entities; variety communicates different threat environments

environmental_storytelling.technique: Star platform mechanic as narrative accessibility
environmental_storytelling.example: Star attack required to reach hidden magic items in each stage; star platform doubles as both combat tool and traversal mechanism, reinforcing Yumetaro's unique capability as rescue hero
Magic items hidden in stage alcoves and secret areas; their collection serves as both gameplay progression gate and narrative reward for thorough exploration
Intro depicts girl's birthday and toy abduction; bad ending loops to start; good ending shows castle collapse and girl rescue, framing player agency through visual narrative bookends
Score and status displayed in lower screen portion mimicking arcade layout; upper gameplay area; visual presentation reinforces arcade-style action communication

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Horn glows; star projectiles spawn above head on input; character oriented toward threats
Star solidifies into platform; character can stand on own generated projectile to reach high areas
Consistent movement pattern; animation loops; no direct player targeting
Knockback animation; damage visual feedback; potential despawn or defeat state
Larger sprite with complex animation patterns; projectile attacks or movement patterns requiring player response; health reduction communicated through visual changes
Glow/shimmer effect; stationary position; distinct visual separation from enemies and terrain
Disappears from level; progress toward good ending increments; hidden stage unlock condition moves closer to satisfaction

notes: Mr. Gimmick is a single-player platformer without faction conflict or political systems. World-state communication relies on environmental progression and visual aesthetic shifts (toy environments → castle decay). Danger is communicated primarily through enemy design diversity and increasing environmental hazard density across stage progression. The good ending / bad ending mechanic creates a binary world state distinction based on player resource management (magic item collection without continues). The game's narrative motivation (rescuing the girl) is communicated through opening cinematic and sprite/environment design rather than in-game dialogue. Anti-tampering routine in Japanese version represents developer intent expression through technical means. Star attack mechanic serves dual narrative/mechanical purpose as Yumetaro's unique identification.
